summary: I have been working at the Barnett National Bank for five years now, managing the bond department. The bank is the oldest banking institution in Jacksonville, and I take pride in being associated with it. My work involves long hours and great responsibility, often extending into the night during busy periods. Despite the challenges, I strive to make good on my role here, as if I were the first Mitchell in the country with no ancestral background to rely on.

My great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, was a financial power in Wisconsin during the late 1860s. Although I know little about my family's early history there, his activities in that state are well documented. Unfortunately, all family papers have been lost, so I rely on historical records to understand his impact. Wisconsin was the place where my ancestor made his mark in finance and railroads.

Milwaukee is where my great-grandfather settled and conducted much of his financial work. Though I have no personal documents to confirm our family's arrival there, his involvement in the city's development is noted in various records. Milwaukee was central to his role as an organizer and leader in banking and railroads. It's a place tied closely to my family's legacy, even if I know little about it firsthand.

Alexander Mitchell was my great-grandfather and a significant figure in American finance during the late 19th century. He was the first president of the American Bankers' Association and an organizer of the Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad. He also founded the Northeastern National Insurance Company in 1869. Though I never knew him personally, his legacy looms large in my family history and the financial world.

My great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, was the first president of the American Bankers Association. This organization was an important part of his financial career in the late 1800s. I respect the role he played in shaping banking standards nationally through this association. Though I work in banking today, I have not reached the same level of influence as he did.

entity: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad
summary: Alexander Mitchell, my great-grandfather, was an organizer and stockholder of the Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad. This railroad was a significant enterprise in the 19th century, contributing to the development of the Midwest. His involvement in this company highlights his diverse interests beyond banking. I see this as part of the broad financial legacy he left behind.

The Northeastern National Insurance Company was founded by my great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, in 1869. He served as its first president, demonstrating his leadership in the insurance industry as well as banking and railroads. This company was part of his extensive financial ventures during his lifetime. Though I have no direct connection to the company today, it remains a notable part of my family's history.

I once kept a newspaper clipping of a write-up about Villa Alexandria by Harriet Beecher Stowe, but unfortunately, I have misplaced it. Stowe's writing gave me some insight into the Villa, which is connected to my family's history. Though I no longer have the clipping, her work remains a valuable cultural reference related to my heritage. I wish I could find it again to learn more.

Jacksonville is where I currently live and work, specifically at the Barnett National Bank. It's also where my father, David Mitchell, has been living, though I haven't seen him for over a year. The city is home to the oldest banking institution with which I am proud to be associated. Jacksonville is central to my present life and professional career.

I attended Duval County Public Schools during my early education before moving on to private schooling. These schools formed the foundation of my academic life here in Jacksonville. My time there was important in preparing me for higher education and my eventual career in banking. I look back on those years as the start of my educational journey.

I graduated from the University of Florida, which was a significant milestone in my education. My studies there helped prepare me for my career in finance and banking. The university provided me with the knowledge and skills I needed to succeed in my current role at the Barnett National Bank. It remains an important part of my personal and professional development.

Currently, I am studying a special course in finance through the American Institute of Banking. This education complements my work at the Barnett National Bank and helps me improve my professional skills. I dedicate much of my off-hours to these studies, aiming to enhance my expertise in banking. The institute plays a key role in my ongoing career development.

I admire President Franklin D. Roosevelt's ideals and believe that in time he will improve the finances, labor conditions, and living standards of the American people. Though I am not involved in politics myself, I respect his leadership and vision during these challenging times. His policies influence how I see the future of our country and its economy.

Bainbridge Richardson works in the Blum Building in Jacksonville, and he might know more about my father’s current whereabouts. I have not seen my father, David Mitchell, for over a year, and Mr. Richardson could provide some clues. He is someone connected to my family’s circle here in Jacksonville. I have spoken with him about my family history and my father’s situation.

Rose Shepherd is the writer who interviewed me and documented my story. She met me at the Barnett National Bank and helped capture my reflections on my family and work. Her role was important in preserving the details of my life and my great-grandfather’s legacy. I appreciate her interest in my history and career.

The Blum Building in Jacksonville is where Bainbridge Richardson has his office. It is a place connected to my family through Mr. Richardson, who might have information about my father. The building is part of the local business environment where some of my family’s associates work. It represents a link to my father’s recent history.

David Mitchell is my father, and I have not seen him for over a year. He was the son of Alexander Mitchell and had a privileged upbringing with every advantage money could buy, including education and travel. Unfortunately, he suffered a serious injury from a fall at Villa Alexandria in his youth, which affected his behavior. He has been somewhat unreliable and difficult, and I do not know his current whereabouts.

Villa Alexandria was a significant place in my family history, notably where my father, David Mitchell, had a severe accident in his youth. He fell from a pole on the grounds and suffered serious injuries, including a fractured skull. I once had a newspaper clipping about the Villa by Harriet Beecher Stowe, which I have since misplaced. The Villa remains a symbol of both family heritage and the challenges my father faced.
